note:
新的竞标，以及新一轮1亿美元的融资，据报道估值为35亿美元，这将有助于该公司抹去其作为游戏玩家之家(以及白人民族主义者的虚拟市政厅)的遗产。
现在，该公司有更多的资金可以用来监控其用户基础，并宣传这项服务不仅仅是为游戏玩家提供的形象。联合创始人杰森·西特伦和斯坦尼斯拉夫·维什涅夫斯基在一篇博客文章中写道：“事实证明，对你们中的许多人来说，这不仅仅是关于视频游戏。”
这两个人将他们的公司描述为“一个在自己的社区和朋友中舒适地闲逛和交谈的地方”。他们说，“不和谐”是“一个与人真诚交谈、共度美好时光的地方，无论是追赶、学习还是分享想法。”
情况并不总是这样。大约三年前，该公司试图引导一些最具种族主义色彩的用户，但他们利用该平台传播仇恨言论的能力一直顽固地存在。直到2019年年中，白人民族主义者还对使用这项服务感到足够舒服，以至于有理由让Daily Stormer创始人安德鲁·安格林(Andrew Anglin)大喊一声，他敦促其他旅行者停止使用这项服务。
“在这些极右翼群体中，不和谐总是存在的，”数据与通信协会研究所(Data&Amp；Society Research Institute)媒体操纵首席研究员琼·多诺万(Joan Donovan)告诉“石板报”(Slate)。“这是他们组织斗牛和骚扰活动最多的地方。”
不和谐说，这些用户只是用户群中的一小部分(而且正在减少)，现在还包括黑人生命也是组织者、社交媒体影响力者，当然还有游戏玩家。
根据该公司的一份声明，该服务目前有超过1亿活跃用户，在670万台活跃服务器上花费40亿分钟进行对话。
如果说有什么不同的话，那就是不和谐的成功既是社交游戏和社交媒体迅速崛起的一个功能和特点。该公司的服务器实现了跨游戏平台的实时通信，使其成为一代玩家的主导社交体验。他们还使各种社交媒体平台上的有影响力的人与他们的粉丝有了更直接的关系。
正如泰勒·洛伦茨(Taylor Lorenz)在报道“不和谐”在社交媒体创业者和名人中新获得的粉丝基础时指出的那样：
去年3月，世界上最受欢迎的视频游戏直播节目之一的忍者教会了德雷克在扮演堡垒之夜时如何使用不和谐。YouTube一线明星如菲利普·德弗兰科(Philip DeFranco)、格蕾丝·赫尔比格(Grace HelBig)和Try Guys都有自己的服务器，而那些致力于讨论“单身汉”和“真正的家庭主妇”的人也有自己的不和谐团体。超过2亿人使用这项服务。
“我们设计了不和谐的谈话方式。没有无休止的滚动，没有新闻馈送，也没有喜欢的跟踪。没有算法决定你“应该”看到什么。我们设计了“不和谐”，以实现我们想要重现的体验和感受：与你的社区和朋友在一起。你已经把你的服务器变成了个人空间，里面挤满了你邀请的人，并设定了讨论的主题，“创始人写道。
更友善、更温和的不和掩盖了公司的名称和根源。但这是一个迹象，表明它在努力改变投资者的看法，并吸引潜在的新用户使用这项服务。
除了新的现金，该公司还强调了新的用户体验，并增加了服务器视频，以便用户可以更方便地进行交流。有模板可以帮助用户创建服务器，该公司已将其语音和视频容量提高了200%。
作为对产品的新关注的一部分，Discord已经启动了它所谓的“安全中心”，该中心明确定义了公司的规章制度，以及用户可以采取什么行动来监控和管理他们使用仇恨言论和辱骂服务的情况。
创始人写道：“我们将继续采取果断行动，打击白人至上主义者、种族主义者和其他试图利用不和为恶的人。”
指数风险投资公司(Index Ventures)的联合创始人丹尼·里默(Danny Rmer)是该公司扩大自身愿景的倡导者。指数风险投资公司领导的投资集团投资了Discorde最新的1亿美元现金注入。
里默在一份声明中写道：“我相信不和谐是平台的未来，因为它展示了一个负责任的网站可以如何为有共同兴趣的人提供一个安全的空间。”“它为你和你的朋友提供了一种共享体验，而不是像Facebook那样向你扔原始内容。我们将逐渐认识到，不和谐对社交谈话的影响，就像斯莱克对专业谈话所做的那样。“。
与Slake的相似之处很有趣，首先是两家公司都是从游戏工作室起步，然后转向通信服务。
“今年在法国，在政府官方服务失败后，”不和谐“被采纳为远程教育的首要APP。因此，不和谐在3月份登上了法国应用程序下载量前十名，今天仍在美国和英国的前50名之列，“里默在解释Index的最新投资时指出。随着Discord计划下一阶段的发展，它将变得更加包容和欢迎新用户和社区，它将继续受到从一开始就通知它发展的用户的指导。“
note_en:
The new pitch, and a new funding round of $100 million at  a reported valuation of $3.5 billion, will help the company as it looks to erase its legacy as a home for gamers (and a virtual  townhall for white nationalists).
  Now, the company has more money at its disposal to monitor its user base and promote the image that the service isn’t just for gamers. “It turns out that, for a lot of you, it wasn’t just about video games anymore,” write co-founders Jason Citron and Stanislav Vishnevskiy  in a blog post.
 The two men frame their company as “a place designed to hang out and talk in the comfort of your own communities and friends.” Discord, they say, is “a place to have genuine conversations and spend quality time with people, whether catching up, learning something or sharing ideas.”
 It hadn’t always been that way. Three years ago, the company tried to boot a number of its most racist users, but their ability to use the platform to disseminate hate speech  has stubbornly persisted. Up until mid-2019 white nationalists were comfortable enough using the service to warrant a shoutout from Daily Stormer founder, Andrew Anglin,  who urged his fellow travelers to stop using the service.
 “Discord is always on and always present among these groups on the far-right,” Joan Donovan, the lead researcher on media manipulation at the Data & Society Research Institute, told Slate. “It’s the place where they do most of the organizing of doxing and harassment campaigns.”
 Discord says these users are a small (and dwindling) fraction of a user base that now also includes Black Lives Matter organizers, social media influencers, and, of course, gamers.
 There are now more than 100 million active users on the service that spend 4 billion minutes in conversation on 6.7 million active servers, according to a statement from the company.
 If anything, Discord’s success is both a function and feature of the rapid rise of social gaming and social media. The company’s servers enabled real time communication across gaming platforms that turned them into the dominant social experience for a generation of players. They also enabled influencers on a variety of social media platforms to have a more direct relationship with their fans.
 As Taylor Lorenz  noted in her reporting on Discord’s newfound fanbase among social media entrepreneurs and celebrities:
 Last March,  Ninja, one of the most popular video-game live-streamers in the world,  taught Drake how to use Discord while playing Fortnite. YouTube A-listers such as Philip DeFranco, Grace Helbig, and the Try Guys all have their own servers, and subreddits such as those dedicated to discussing   The Bachelor and   The Real Housewives have their own Discord groups too. More than 200 million people use the service.
 “We designed Discord for talking. There’s no endless scrolling, no news feed, and no tracking likes. No algorithms decide what you ‘should’ see. We designed Discord to enable the experience and feelings we wanted to recreate: being together with your community and friends. You’ve made your servers into personal spaces filled with people you invited and set the topics of discussion,” the founders write.
 The kinder, gentler Discord belies both the company’s name and its roots. But it is a sign of its efforts to shift the perceptions of investors and woo potential new users to the service.
 In addition to its new cash, the company is highlighting a new user experience and added server video so that users can communicate more readily. There are templates available to help users create servers, and the company has increased its voice and video capacity by 200%.
 As part of this new focus on product, Discord has launched what it calls a “ Safety Center” which clearly defines the company’s rules and regulations and what actions users can take to monitor and manage their use of service for hate speech and abuse.
 “ We will continue to take decisive action against white supremacists, racists, and others who seek to use Discord for evil,” the founders write.
 Danny Rimer, the co-founder of Index Ventures, which led the investor group that invested in Discord’s latest $100 million cash infusion, is an advocate for the company’s expanded vision for itself.
 “I believe Discord is the future of platforms because it demonstrates how a responsibly curated site can provide a safe space for people with shared interests,” Rimer  wrote in a statement. “Rather than throwing raw content at you, like Facebook, it provides a shared experience for you and your friends. We’ll come to appreciate that Discord does for social conversation what Slack has done for professional conversation.”
  The parallels to Slack are interesting and begin with the fact that both companies began their lives as gaming studios before moving to become communications services.
 “In France this year, Discord was adopted as the primary app for distance learning after the government’s official service failed. As a result, Discord reached the top ten app downloads in France in March and is still in the top 50 in the US and UK today,” Rimer noted in his explanation of Index’s latest investment. “As Discord plans its next phase of growth, it will become even more inclusive and welcoming for new users and communities and it will continue to be guided by the users that have informed its development from the start.”
title: “不和谐”现在的估值为35亿美元，游戏中的销售宣传较轻，估值为1亿美元。
title_en: Discord now has a $3.5B valuation and $100M for a sales pitch lighter on the gaming

note:
虽然这本质上是一个长期的意向声明，以帮助合作伙伴诺斯罗普·格鲁曼公司(Northrop Grumman)确保其供应管道能够在NASA所需的时间表内供应额外的助推器，而不是实际订购助推器本身，但这仍然是一大步，潜在的合同总价值为4950万美元，初步资金现在已经解锁。目前的时间表要求这些额外的助推器在2030年12月31日之前的某个时候交付，以提供关于阿耳特弥斯任务4-9可能何时真正飞行的某种想法。
固体助推器与SLS(太空发射系统)成对使用，SLS核心级两侧各一个固体助推器，提供其发射起飞阶段总推力的75%左右。它们的设计基本上借鉴了航天飞机计划中使用的那些，但增加了吸引力，以帮助更重、更大的SLS离开地球大气层进入太空。
NASA还在采购新的RS-25发动机和在Artemis计划中执行任务所需的核心级，超出最初的三级，并继续为Artemis 1号做准备，火箭组装过程将在助推器几乎准备好堆叠的时候进行。Artemis 1目前的目标是在2021年11月发射。
note_en:
While this is essentially a long-lead declaration of intent to help partner  Northrop Grumman   ensure its supply pipeline can supply the additional boosters in a timeline needed by NASA, rather than an actual order for the boosters themselves, it’s still a big step with a potential total contract value of $49.5 million, with initial funding unlocked now. The schedule currently calls for those additional boosters to be delivered sometime before December 31, 2030, to provide some kind of idea about when Artemis missions 4-9 might potentially actually fly.
 Solid boosters are used with the  SLS   (Space Launch System) in pairs, with one on each side flanking the SLS Core stage to provide around 75% of the total thrust power used during the take-off phase of its launch. They essentially take their design from those used during the Space Shuttle program, but with added oomph to help the heavier and larger SLS out of Earth’s atmosphere and into space.
  NASA has also been in the process of procuring new RS-25 engines and core stages needed for missions in the Artemis program beyond the initial three, and continues to prepare for Artemis 1, with the rocket assembly process for that at the point where the boosters are nearly ready to stack. Artemis 1 is currently targeting a November 2021 timeframe for launch.
title: NASA通过SLS助推器订单为最多六次Artemis月球探测任务铺平道路
title_en: NASA sets path for up to six more Artemis moon exploration missions with SLS booster order

note:
一个例子是：几十年来，美国在纯禁欲项目上投资了数十亿美元。最终，学校拒绝了政府对这些项目的资助，并推动了一个更全面、更医学上准确的议程。即使取得了进步，全国各地的学校仍在继续处理不准确的遗留问题。政府仍在资助仅限节欲的项目。
这对学生来说是个坏消息，而对于Lessonbee Reva McPollom的创始人来说，早就应该做出改变了。她可以亲自证明健康课上不全面的教育会如何孤立学生。
麦克波伦说，当她还是个孩子的时候，她被称为假小子，因为她认为自己是女性，所以感到困惑。没有传授性别陈规定型观念和规范的危险的教训。
她说：“我觉得不应该喜欢运动，不应该想打鼓，我不应该做我喜欢或喜欢的事情，也不应该把自己作为身份认同的一部分。”
她说，这种无声的痛苦一直持续到高中：“如果你看我的毕业年鉴，就好像我根本不在里面一样，那时我就把自己完全抹去了。”
note_en:
One example:  For decades, the United States invested billions into abstinence-only programs. Eventually, schools rejected government funding for these programs and pushed a more comprehensive and medically accurate agenda. Even with progress, schools across the country continue to reckon with a legacy of inaccuracy. And the government  is still funding abstinence-only programs.
 It’s bad news for students, and for founder of  Lessonbee  Reva McPollom, a change is long overdue. She can personally vouch for how non-comprehensive education in health classes can isolate students.
  As a child, McPollom said she was called a tomboy and felt confused because she identified as a female. There was no lesson teaching the danger of gender stereotypes and norms.
 “I felt wrong for liking sports, for wanting to play drums, I felt wrong for everything that I loved or liked or attached myself too as part of my identity,” she said.
 The silent suffering, she says, continued through high school: “If you look at my senior yearbook, like I’m not even in it, I just totally erased myself by that point.”
title: 健康课已经过时了，所以Lessonbee想要修复它
title_en: Health class is outdated, so Lessonbee wants to fix it

note:
据“纽约时报”报道，在优步与外卖公司GrubHub的合并谈判破裂后，优步现在将目光投向了Postmate。叫车服务公司Uber Eats的外卖部门Uber Eats和Uber的其他业务一样处于亏损状态，但这并没有阻止该公司据报道出价26亿美元收购PostMates。
优步(Uber)一直在寻找在疫情爆发期间维持运营的方法，因为其核心叫车业务已经崩溃，其将司机员工错误归类为独立承包商以节省劳动力成本的商业模式正在加州和全国范围内受到越来越严格的审查。在第一季度财报电话会议上，优步报告称，乘车次数下降了80%以上，亏损29亿美元，令人垂涎(它从未记录过盈利)，但也有一个亮点：自去年以来，外卖增加了54%。
不过，目前还不清楚优步Eats或收购PostMates是否足以拯救公司。今年3月，加州一家基于APP的司机倡导组织Rideshare Drivers United发布了一个工资索赔工具，让司机可以索赔被盗的工资和未支付的商业费用；仅有4000名优步和Lyft司机提出了超过10亿美元的索赔。去年，优步和Lyft的司机人数远远超过50万，有报道称，优步的年度司机保留率约为4%。请注意，这只在加利福尼亚州发生，而且只包括工资索赔-要求优步不仅在加州，而且在美国其他地区缴纳州失业保险税的呼声也越来越高，如果这一前景成为现实，可能会额外花费数十亿美元。
面对如此糟糕的数字，与GrubHub的合并将是一剂非常受欢迎的强心针。GrubHub占据了美国外卖市场约34%的份额，而UberEats只有24%。那么，当荷兰外卖服务公司JustEatsTakeaway收购GrubHub并宣布将“在中国以外创建世界上最大的外卖业务”时，优步的垄断梦想破灭了，这太糟糕了。
优步现在对Postmate感兴趣，Postmate只拥有美国8%的市场份额，现在将不得不决定是上市还是冒着可能引发反垄断担忧的收购的风险。优步在这里的表现可能还不够大，不足以阻止小鸡回家栖息-特别是因为Postmate的业务集中在加州，那里的司法部长泽维尔·贝塞拉(Xavier Becera)可能会迫使零工平台将他们的司机正确归类为员工。
这在很大程度上让人感觉像是在重新安排泰坦尼克号上的甲板椅。优步尝试了许多赚钱的方式：拼车、自行车共享、自动驾驶汽车和送餐。这一切都没有奏效。它的新想法似乎是收购同样亏损的竞争对手，并希望这能改变一些事情。目前还不清楚这将如何运作，但目前足以让投资者满意。

note_en:
After Uber's merger talks with food-delivery company Grubhub fell apart, Uber has now set its sights on Postmates,  according to the New York Times. Uber Eats, the ride-hailing company's food-delivery unit, is just as unprofitable as the rest of Uber's business operations, but that hasn’t stopped the company from reportedly offering $2.6 billion to takeover Postmates.
  Uber has been searching for ways to stay afloat during the pandemic as its  core ride-hailing business has collapsed and its business model of  misclassifying driver-employees as independent contractors to save on labor costs is coming under increased scrutiny in  California and  nationwide. In its Q1 earnings call, Uber reported that rides were down over 80 percent and it had recorded an eye-watering loss of $2.9 billion (it has never recorded a profit), but there was a bright spot: food-delivery was up by 54 percent since last year.
  Still, it’s not clear that Uber Eats—or an acquisition of Postmates—will be enough to save the company. In March, Rideshare Drivers United, an app-based driver advocacy group in California, released  a wage claim tool to let drivers claim stolen wages and unpaid business expenses; a mere 4,000 Uber and Lyft drivers have filed claims  in excess of $1 billion. Last year, there were well over  half a million Uber and Lyft drivers last year and reports have pegged Uber’s annual driver retention rate at around  4 percent. Mind you, this is only in California and only includes wage claims—there is also a growing call for  Uber to pay state unemployment insurance taxes in not only California but the rest of the country, a prospect that could cost billions more if realized.
  Facing such abysmal numbers, a merger with Grubhub—which holds about 34 percent of the US market share in food delivery, compared to UberEats’ 24 percent—would've been a much welcome shot in the arm. Too bad, then, that Uber’s monopoly dreams were dashed when the Dutch food-delivery service JustEatsTakeaway  bought Grubhub and announced it would “create the world’s largest food delivery business outside China.”
  Uber is now interested in Postmates, which  only enjoys 8 percent of the US market share and will now have to decide between  going public or risking an acquisition that could raise antitrust concerns. Uber’s play here probably isn’t big enough to stop the chickens from coming home to roost—especially since Postmates’ business is  concentrated in California where Attorney General Xavier Becerra may force gig platforms to  properly classify their drivers as employees
  Much of this feels like rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic. Uber has tried many things to make money: rideshare, bikeshare, self-driving cars, and food delivery. None of it has worked. Its new idea seems to be buying competitors  who also lose money, and hoping that changes something. It’s not clear how this will work, but it’s enough to keep investors happy for now.

title: 优步的新战略：收购无利可图的公司，盈利
title_en: Uber's New Strategy: Buy Unprofitable Companies, ???, Profit

note:
阿拉巴马州蒙哥马利(路透社)-法官莱斯·海耶斯(Les Hayes)曾因未能支付交通罚单而判处一名单身母亲496天监禁。判决是如此严厉，以至于超过了阿拉巴马州允许过失杀人的监禁时间。
2012年4月被关押的马奎塔·约翰逊表示，她在监狱中的影响一直持续到今天。约翰逊被监禁期间，她的三个孩子被寄养。州记录显示，一个女儿被猥亵。另一人受到身体虐待。
现年36岁的约翰逊说：“海耶斯法官夺走了我的生命，并不关心我的孩子们遭受的痛苦。”“我的女儿们永远都不会一样了。”
狱友们觉得她的判决令人难以置信。约翰逊说：“他们给我起了个绰号：整天呆着的女人。”“他们就是这么叫我的：整天呆着的女人。有一些真正犯罪的人比我早出来了。“。
2016年，负责监督法官的州机构指控海耶斯违反阿拉巴马州的司法行为准则。根据司法调查委员会的说法，海耶斯将约翰逊和其他数百名太穷而无法支付罚款的蒙哥马利居民监禁起来，违反了州和联邦法律。在那些被监禁的人中：一名努力挣钱交房租的水管工，一名为了支付残疾儿子的医药费而不吃饭的母亲，以及一名靠读完大学而打工的酒店管家。
海耶斯自2000年以来一直担任法官，他在法庭文件中承认违反了该州司法行为准则的10个不同部分。其中一项指控是违反了法官最基本的职责：未能“尊重和遵守法律”。
尽管裁决很严厉，但海耶斯并未被禁止担任法官。取而代之的是，司法委员会和海耶斯达成了一项协议。这位前鹰级童子军将被无偿停职11个月。然后他就可以回到替补席上了。
在受到纪律处分之前，海耶斯在接受路透采访时表示，“我从未认为我做错了什么.”
本周，海耶斯将在担任法官20年后退休。在给路透社的一份声明中，海耶斯表示，他对自己的不当行为“非常懊悔”。
社区积极分子说，他早就应该离开了。然而，他们说，考虑到他的不当行为记录，离开的决定永远不应该由他做出。
北蒙哥马利浸信会牧师威利·奈特(Willie Knight)说：“他几年前就应该被解雇了。”“他触犯了法律，想逍遥法外。他的突然退休太晚了好几年。“。
路透社(Reuters)的一项调查发现，全美数以千计的州和地方法官在违反司法道德规则或违反他们承诺维护的法律后，被允许保留拥有非凡权力和威望的职位，海耶斯就是其中之一。
法官发表种族主义声明，对州官员撒谎，迫使被告在没有律师的情况下在监狱里受苦受难-然后回到法官席上，有时除了来自监督他们行为的州机构的指责之外，几乎没有什么更多的东西。
最近的媒体报道记录了南卡罗来纳州、路易斯安那州和伊利诺伊州在司法监督方面的失败。路透社走得更远。
在对全国司法不当行为的首次全面统计中，路透社(Reuters)审查了过去十几年-2008年至2019年-法官辞职、退休或在受到不当行为指控后受到公开纪律处分的1509起案件。此外，记者还确认了从2008年到2018年的另外3613起案件，在这些案件中，各州对任性的法官进行了纪律处分，但对他们犯罪的公开关键细节-包括法官本身的身份-隐瞒了出来。
路透社(Reuters)确定，总共有9名法官在因不当行为而受到制裁后，每10名法官中就有9名被允许重返法官席。其中包括一名加州法官，他在自己的法院房间里发生了性行为，一次是与他的前法律实习生发生性关系，另一次是与一名律师发生性关系；一名纽约法官斥责了家庭暴力受害者；还有一名马里兰州法官，他因酒后驾车被捕后，只要在每次出庭前接受酒精测试，就可以回到法官席上。
纽约大学(New York University)法学教授斯蒂芬·吉勒斯(Stephen Gillers)写过一篇关于司法伦理的文章，他说，该通讯社的调查结果揭示了一个“过度”宽容的司法纪律体系。吉勒说，尽管不罢免法官的处罚对大多数不当行为案件来说是合适的，但公众对法官对重大违法行为获得的一些宽松待遇“会感到震惊”。
在犹他州，一名法官向法院书记员发送了一段男子阴囊的视频。他受到了斥责，但仍坐在替补席上。
在印第安纳州，去年春天参加一次会议的三名评委喝醉了，引发了凌晨3点的骚乱。一家白色城堡快餐店外发生斗殴，最终两名法官被枪杀。尽管州最高法院认定这三名法官“败坏了整个印第安纳州司法机构的声誉”，但他们在被停职后都回到了法官席上。
在德克萨斯州，一名法官闯入陪审员审议一名被指控性交易的妇女的案件，并宣布上帝告诉他被告是无辜的。犯法的法官收到警告后又回到法官席上。在一名新法官接手此案后，被告被判有罪。
陪审团组长马克·豪斯(Mark House)告诉路透社(Reuters)：“有些事情应该是零容忍的。”豪斯说，法官应该被罚款，并被踢出法官席。“没有正义，因为他还在做他的工作。”
司法不端行为专家说，这样的行为有可能侵蚀人们对美国法院的信任，如果没有严重的后果，可能会让法官有权行事而不受惩罚。
“当你看到这样的案例时，公众开始怀疑这个系统的正直和诚实，”路易斯安那州监督机构负责人、曾担任纽约监督机构副主任的律师史蒂夫·萨克曼(Steve Scheckman)说。“这看起来像是一家不错的老男孩俱乐部。”
这就是当地律师对阿拉巴马州一名长期担任该州司法监督委员会法官的案件的看法。法官，卡尔曼地区法院的金·钱尼(Kim Chaney)，在被指控违反了他在监督委员会执行的同样的裙带关系规则后，继续担任法官长达三年之久。法庭记录显示，在至少200起案件中，钱尼法官选择了自己的儿子担任法院指定的穷人辩护律师，使小钱尼能够在两年内赚取至少10.5万美元的费用。
今年2月，在路透社多次询问钱尼和州司法委员会有关这些案件的几个月后，作为与州当局结束调查的协议的一部分，他从法官席上退休。
2016年首次对切尼提起诉讼的律师汤米·德雷克(Tommy Drake)表示，如果路透社没有审查此案，他怀疑法官是否会被迫下台。
德雷克说：“你知道他们对钱尼采取行动的唯一原因是因为你们开始问问题。”“否则，他还会在那里。”
州和地方法官几乎没有受到审查，尽管他们的法庭是美国刑事司法系统的基石，每年都会影响数百万人的生活。
该国约1700名联邦法官每年审理40万起案件。近3万名州、县和市法院法官处理的案件要多得多：从交通到离婚再到谋杀，每年都有超过1亿起新案件。他们的头衔从治安大法官到州最高法院大法官不一而足。他们的权力巨大而多样-从决定被告是否应该入狱到决定谁应该得到孩子的监护权。
美国每个州都有一个监督机构，调查针对法官的不当行为投诉。监督机构的权力与上诉法院拥有的权力截然不同，上诉法院可以推翻法官的法律裁决，下令重新审判。司法委员会不能改变判决。相反，他们可以调查对法官行为的投诉，并采取从谴责到免职的各种纪律。
几乎没有专家质疑绝大多数法官的行为是负责任的，尊重法律和那些出现在他们面前的人。一些人认为，当法官确实犹豫不决时，监督机构在识别和解决这种行为方面是有效的。亚利桑那大学专门研究司法伦理学的法学教授基思·斯威舍(Keith Swisher)说，“除了少数几个值得注意的例外，这些委员会通常都是正确的。”
其他人则不同意。他们指出，这些委员会的影响力是有限的，它们的权力因州而异。要罢免一名法官，除少数州外，所有州都需要包括其他法官在内的小组的批准。而且大多数州很少充分行使这些纪律权力。
因此，制度往往偏向于保护法官的权利和声誉，而忽视了法庭不当行为对受影响最严重的人的影响：像马奎塔·约翰逊(Marquita Johnson)这样的人。
路透社查阅了过去十几年的数千份州调查文件、纪律程序和法庭记录，以量化司法不当行为造成的个人伤亡。检查发现，至少有5206人直接受到法官不当行为的影响。纪律文件中引用的受害者从被非法监禁的人到那些受到法官以玷污案件的方式发表的种族主义、性别歧视和其他辱骂言论的人。
这个数字是保守估计。这一数字不包括之前报道的两起事件，这两起事件影响了数千名被告，并促使人们对司法行为进行全面审查。
在宾夕法尼亚州，该州审查了被两名法官判刑的3500多名青少年的定罪情况。法官们因收受回扣而被判有罪，这是填补私人青少年拘留中心计划的一部分。2009年，宾夕法尼亚州最高法院任命高级法官阿瑟·格里姆(Arthur Grim)领导受害者审查，该州后来删除了2251名青少年的犯罪记录。格里姆告诉路透社，每个州都应该采取一种方式来补偿司法不当行为的受害者。
格里姆说：“如果我们有一个追究违法者责任的制度，但我们没有解决受害者的问题，那么我们真的就看不到司法制度应该是什么了。”
在俄亥俄州正在进行的另一项审查中，州公设辩护律师蒂姆·杨(Tim Young)正在审查一名法官处理的2707起案件，这名法官在因酗酒住院后于2018年退休。凯斯西储大学(Case Western Reserve University)法学教授迈克·本扎(Mike Benza)的学生正在帮助识别受害者，他将这项工作与目前对警察滥用权力的调查相提并论。他说：“你看到一个病例，然后再看看它是否是系统性的。”
这项审查在冠状病毒大流行期间一直受到限制，可能需要一年时间。但是杨说，这项耗时的任务是必不可少的，因为“可能对成百上千的人施加了根本的不公正”。
大多数州为被控行为不端的法官提供一种温和的司法。也许没有哪个州比阿拉巴马州更能说明美国法官监督制度的缺陷。
与大多数州一样，阿拉巴马州的司法调查委员会由九名成员组成，由律师、法官和非专业人士组成。所有人都是被任命的。他们的审议是秘密的，他们在全国一些对法官最友好的规则下运作。
阿拉巴马州的规定使得甚至很难对法官提出申诉。申诉必须经过公证，这意味着从理论上讲，任何对法官做出错误陈述的人都可以以伪证罪被起诉。对不当行为的投诉必须以书面形式提出；那些通过电话、电子邮件或没有公证章到达的投诉不会受到调查，尽管发件人会被告知他们的投诉被草率拒绝的原因。匿名的书面投诉会被粉碎。
委员会主任珍妮·加勒特(Jenny Garrett)在回答书面问题时指出，这些规则可能会让律师和诉讼人担心遭到报复。
2007年至2011年担任阿拉巴马州最高法院首席大法官的苏·贝尔·科布(Sue Bell Cobb)表示：“这是一种保护法官的荒谬制度，让他们很容易用合法的控诉来恐吓任何人。”2009年，她倡导对这一过程进行改革，但未获成功，并委托美国律师协会(American Bar Association)撰写了一份报告，对阿拉巴马州的规则进行了严厉的审查。
在大多数其他州，委员会工作人员可以在接到电话或电子邮件，甚至是匿名电话或电子邮件后，或者在从新闻报道或法庭文件中了解到可疑行为后，开始调查法官。在阿拉巴马州，工作人员在没有委员会本身批准的情况下不会开始调查，委员会大约每七周召开一次会议。
根据规定，委员会还必须在整个调查过程中让一名接受审查的法官充分了解情况。如果发出传票，法官会同时收到一份副本，这引发了人们对证人恐吓的担忧。如果证人向调查人员提供证词，法官就会收到笔录。在美国司法系统中，这种对被调查个人的尊重是极其罕见的。
“为什么需要为法官制定特殊规则？”曾在备受瞩目的刑事、公司、国会和证券调查中代表客户的华盛顿律师迈克尔·利维(Michael Levy)说。“如果法官认为在不向那些主体或目标提供证人陈述和传票副本的情况下调查他人的犯罪或不当行为是公平和适当的，为什么法官不认为以同样的方式调查法官是公平的呢？”
阿拉巴马州的法官也有机会秘密解决调查。路透社(Reuters)的采访和对阿拉巴马州委员会记录的审查显示，自2011年以来，该委员会至少与法官非正式会面19次，以提供纠正的“指导”。这些法官的身份仍然保密，促使会议的行为也是如此。委员会主任加勒特说：“并不是每一次违规行为都需要纪律处分。”
自2008年以来，该委员会已经对包括海耶斯在内的21名法官提起了21起公开案件，今年起诉了两名法官。
该委员会提起的两起最著名的案件涉及罗伊·摩尔(Roy Moore)，他曾两次因违抗联邦法院命令而被迫辞去阿拉巴马州最高法院首席大法官的职务。
然而，阿拉巴马州的另一名法官在挑战不当行为投诉方面表现得更好。汤姆·帕克(Tom Parker)于2004年首次当选该州高等法院法官，2015年委员会调查他在电台发表的批评美国最高法院同性婚姻合法化决定的言论时，他进行了反击。
帕克在联邦法院起诉该委员会，辩称该机构侵犯了他的第一修正案权利。他赢了。尽管委员会在裁决前放弃了调查，但它被勒令支付帕克的律师费：10万美元，约占该机构年度预算总额的五分之一。
帕克告诉路透社，这些天，阿拉巴马州的法官和监督他们的机构享受着政治色彩较少的“更好的关系”。“我该怎么说呢？现在委员会和法官之间更加尊重了。“。
阿拉巴马州蒙哥马利有着深厚的种族冲突历史，这反映在该市大印章上的冲突概念上：“邦联的摇篮”和“民权运动的发源地”。
1861年南方脱离联邦后，杰斐逊·戴维斯在这里宣誓就任南部邦联总统，他的生日是州假日。就像整个南方常见的那样，这座城市是黑人男子被私刑的地点，现在这里的一个国家纪念馆纪念这些罪行。1955年，警方在这里逮捕了民权偶像罗莎·帕克斯，因为她拒绝在城市公交车上给一名白人乘客让座。
今天，美国人口普查记录显示，蒙哥马利19.8万居民中约有60%是黑人。路透社(Reuters)对法庭记录的检查发现，即便如此，黑人驾车者在被控未付交通罚单的人中约占90%。海耶斯法官在市政法院的大部分工作涉及交通案件和罚款的收取。海耶斯是白人，他告诉路透社，“来法庭的大多数人都是黑人。”
市政府官员表示，无论是种族还是经济，都没有在警方执行尚未执行的逮捕令的努力中发挥作用，无论罪行多么轻微。
2012年4月，马奎塔·约翰逊(Marquita Johnson)就是其中之一。周三早上，这位28岁的单身母亲出现在海耶斯面前，恳求休息一下。
约翰逊八年来一直在努力支付数十张罚单，这些罚单始于未能出示保险证明的传票。她说，她有保险。但是当她被拦下时，她找不到卡片来证明这一点。
即使是一张罚单也是对她最低工资的女服务员工资的沉重打击。除了罚款，法院还向每张门票收取155美元的费用。法庭记录显示，警方经常在每一次停车期间给她开出多张其他违章行为的罚单-一些居民称这种做法为“堆叠”。
根据州法律，即使没有支付一张罚单也会导致驾驶执照被吊销。尽管如此，约翰逊还是决定继续开车-送孩子上学或看医生，买杂货，上班-导致更多的罚单和更深的债务。
“我告诉海耶斯法官，我丢了工作，需要更多时间来支付，”她回忆道。
根据海耶斯的计算，约翰逊欠下的罚款超过12000美元。他判处约翰逊496天监禁。海耶斯在做出这一判决时，将监狱中的每一天都算作未偿债务的25美元。另一名法官后来裁定，约翰逊实际上欠了海耶斯计算的金额的一半，海耶斯错误地惩罚了她已经支付的罚款。为了减少服刑时间，约翰逊在入狱期间清洗警车并从事其他卑微的劳动。
海耶斯告诉路透社，他通常觉得贫穷的恳求很难相信。海耶斯说：“凭我多年的经验，我能判断出是否有人对我诚实。”他称这是“直觉”--尽管他在本周的一份声明中补充说，他还参考了“每位被告的犯罪和交通历史，以及他们有权出庭和没有出庭的历史。”
当然，法律对法官的要求比直觉更高。在1983年具有里程碑意义的比尔登诉佐治亚州案中，美国最高法院裁定，州法官有义务举行听证会，以确定被告是否“故意”选择不支付罚款。
根据该州司法监督委员会的说法，“海耶斯法官没有对约翰逊女士的支付能力进行任何调查，她的拒付是否是故意的。”
约翰逊说，在监狱里，“我祈祷回到我女儿身边。”“我确信会有人意识到海耶斯犯了一个错误。”
她说，她在监狱里最糟糕的一天是她小女儿的3岁生日。在监狱的电话里，她试图唱“生日快乐”，但在悲痛中摔倒在地板上。
“她哽咽着哭了，”约翰逊的母亲布兰奇(Blanche)说，她当时正在打电话。“离开孩子这么长时间，她感到悲痛欲绝。”

note_en:
MONTGOMERY, Alabama (Reuters) - Judge Les Hayes once sentenced a single mother to 496 days behind bars for failing to pay traffic tickets. The sentence was so stiff it exceeded the jail time Alabama allows for negligent homicide.
  Marquita Johnson, who was locked up in April 2012, says the impact of her time in jail endures today. Johnson’s three children were cast into foster care while she was incarcerated. One daughter was molested, state records show. Another was physically abused.
 “Judge Hayes took away my life and didn’t care how my children suffered,” said Johnson, now 36. “My girls will never be the same.”
 Fellow inmates found her sentence hard to believe. “They had a nickname for me: The Woman with All the Days,” Johnson said. “That’s what they called me: The Woman with All the Days. There were people who had committed real crimes who got out before me.”
 In 2016, the state agency that oversees judges charged Hayes with violating Alabama’s code of judicial conduct. According to the Judicial Inquiry Commission, Hayes broke state and federal laws by jailing Johnson and hundreds of other Montgomery residents too poor to pay fines. Among those jailed: a plumber struggling to make rent, a mother who skipped meals to cover the medical bills of her disabled son, and a hotel housekeeper working her way through college.
 Hayes, a judge since 2000, admitted in court documents to violating 10 different parts of the state’s judicial conduct code. One of the counts was a breach of a judge’s most essential duty: failing to “respect and comply with the law.”
 Despite the severity of the ruling, Hayes wasn’t barred from serving as a judge. Instead, the judicial commission and Hayes reached a deal. The former Eagle Scout would serve an 11-month unpaid suspension. Then he could return to the bench.
 Until he was disciplined, Hayes said in an interview with Reuters, “I never thought I was doing something wrong.”
 This week, Hayes is set to retire after 20 years as a judge. In a statement to Reuters, Hayes said he was “very remorseful” for his misdeeds.
 Community activists say his departure is long overdue. Yet the decision to leave, they say, should never have been his to make, given his record of misconduct.
 “He should have been fired years ago,” said Willie Knight, pastor of North Montgomery Baptist Church. “He broke the law and wanted to get away with it. His sudden retirement is years too late.”
 Hayes is among thousands of state and local judges across America who were allowed to keep positions of extraordinary power and prestige after violating judicial ethics rules or breaking laws they pledged to uphold, a Reuters investigation found.
 Judges have made racist statements, lied to state officials and forced defendants to languish in jail without a lawyer – and then returned to the bench, sometimes with little more than a rebuke from the state agencies overseeing their conduct.
 Recent media reports have documented failures in judicial oversight in South Carolina, Louisiana and Illinois. Reuters went further.
 In the first comprehensive accounting of judicial misconduct nationally, Reuters reviewed 1,509 cases from the last dozen years – 2008 through 2019 – in which judges resigned, retired or were publicly disciplined following accusations of misconduct. In addition, reporters identified another 3,613 cases from 2008 through 2018 in which states disciplined wayward judges but kept hidden from the public key details of their offenses – including the identities of the judges themselves.
 All told, 9 of every 10 judges were allowed to return to the bench after they were sanctioned for misconduct, Reuters determined. They included a California judge who had sex in his courthouse chambers, once with his former law intern and separately with an attorney; a New York judge who berated domestic violence victims; and a Maryland judge who, after his arrest for driving drunk, was allowed to return to the bench provided he took a Breathalyzer test before each appearance.
 The news agency’s findings reveal an “excessively” forgiving judicial disciplinary system, said Stephen Gillers, a law professor at New York University who writes about judicial ethics. Although punishment short of removal from the bench is appropriate for most misconduct cases, Gillers said, the public “would be appalled at some of the lenient treatment judges get” for substantial transgressions.
  In Utah, a judge texted a video of a man’s scrotum to court clerks. He was reprimanded but remains on the bench.
 In Indiana, three judges attending a conference last spring got drunk and sparked a 3 a.m. brawl outside a White Castle fast-food restaurant that ended with two of the judges shot. Although the state supreme court found the three judges had “discredited the entire Indiana judiciary,” each returned to the bench after a suspension.
 In Texas, a judge burst in on jurors deliberating the case of a woman charged with sex trafficking and declared that God told him the defendant was innocent. The offending judge received a warning and returned to the bench. The defendant was convicted after a new judge took over the case.
 “There are certain things where there should be a level of zero tolerance,” the jury foreman, Mark House, told Reuters. The judge should have been fined, House said, and kicked off the bench. “There is no justice, because he is still doing his job.”
 Judicial misconduct specialists say such behavior has the potential to erode trust in America’s courts and, absent tough consequences, could give judges license to behave with impunity.
 “When you see cases like that, the public starts to wonder about the integrity and honesty of the system,” said Steve Scheckman, a lawyer who directed Louisiana’s oversight agency and served as deputy director of New York’s. “It looks like a good ol’ boys club.”
 That’s how local lawyers viewed the case of a longtime Alabama judge who concurrently served on the state’s judicial oversight commission. The judge, Cullman District Court’s Kim Chaney, remained on the bench for three years after being accused of violating the same nepotism rules he was tasked with enforcing on the oversight commission. In at least 200 cases, court records show, Judge Chaney chose his own son to serve as a court-appointed defense lawyer for the indigent, enabling the younger Chaney to earn at least $105,000 in fees over two years.
 In February, months after Reuters repeatedly asked Chaney and the state judicial commission about those cases, he retired from the bench as part of a deal with state authorities to end the investigation.
 Tommy Drake, the lawyer who first filed a complaint against Chaney in 2016, said he doubts the judge would have been forced from the bench if Reuters hadn’t examined the case.
 “You know the only reason they did anything about Chaney is because you guys started asking questions,” Drake said. “Otherwise, he’d still be there.”
  State and local judges draw little scrutiny even though their courtrooms are the bedrock of the American criminal justice system, touching the lives of millions of people every year.
 The country’s approximately 1,700 federal judges hear 400,000 cases annually. The nearly 30,000 state, county and municipal court judges handle a far bigger docket: more than 100 million new cases each year, from traffic to divorce to murder. Their titles range from justice of the peace to state supreme court justice. Their powers are vast and varied – from determining whether a defendant should be jailed to deciding who deserves custody of a child.
 Each U.S. state has an oversight agency that investigates misconduct complaints against judges. The authority of the oversight agencies is distinct from the power held by appellate courts, which can reverse a judge’s legal ruling and order a new trial. Judicial commissions cannot change verdicts. Rather, they can investigate complaints about the behavior of judges and pursue discipline ranging from reprimand to removal.
 Few experts dispute that the great majority of judges behave responsibly, respecting the law and those who appear before them. And some contend that, when judges do falter, oversight agencies are effective in identifying and addressing the behavior. “With a few notable exceptions, the commissions generally get it right,” said Keith Swisher, a University of Arizona law professor who specializes in judicial ethics.
 Others disagree. They note that the clout of these commissions is limited, and their authority differs from state to state. To remove a judge, all but a handful of states require approval of a panel that includes other judges. And most states seldom exercise the full extent of those disciplinary powers.
 As a result, the system tends to err on the side of protecting the rights and reputations of judges while overlooking the impact courtroom wrongdoing has on those most affected by it: people like Marquita Johnson.
 Reuters scoured thousands of state investigative files, disciplinary proceedings and court records from the past dozen years to quantify the personal toll of judicial misconduct. The examination found at least 5,206 people who were directly affected by a judge’s misconduct. The victims cited in disciplinary documents ranged from people who were illegally jailed to those subjected to racist, sexist and other abusive comments from judges in ways that tainted the cases.
 The number is a conservative estimate. The tally doesn’t include two previously reported incidents that affected thousands of defendants and prompted sweeping reviews of judicial conduct.
 In Pennsylvania, the state examined the convictions of more than 3,500 teenagers sentenced by two judges. The judges were convicted of taking kickbacks as part of a scheme to fill a private juvenile detention center. In 2009, the Pennsylvania Supreme Court appointed senior judge Arthur Grim to lead a victim review, and the state later expunged criminal records for 2,251 juveniles. Grim told Reuters that every state should adopt a way to compensate victims of judicial misconduct.
 “If we have a system that holds a wrongdoer accountable but we fail to address the victims, then we are really losing sight of what a justice system should be all about,” Grim said.
 In another review underway in Ohio, state public defender Tim Young is scrutinizing 2,707 cases handled by a judge who retired in 2018 after being hospitalized for alcoholism. Mike Benza, a law professor at Case Western Reserve University whose students are helping identify victims, compared the work to current investigations into police abuse of power. “You see one case and then you look to see if it’s systemic,” he said.
 The review, which has been limited during the coronavirus pandemic, may take a year. But Young said the time-consuming task is essential because “a fundamental injustice may have been levied against hundreds or thousands of people.”
  Most states afford judges accused of misconduct a gentle kind of justice. Perhaps no state better illustrates the shortcomings of America’s system for overseeing judges than Alabama.
 As in most states, Alabama’s nine-member Judicial Inquiry Commission is a mix of lawyers, judges and lay people. All are appointed. Their deliberations are secret and they operate under some of the most judge-friendly rules in the nation.
 Alabama’s rules make even filing a complaint against a judge difficult. The complaint must be notarized, which means that in theory, anyone who makes misstatements about the judge can be prosecuted for perjury. Complaints about wrongdoing must be made in writing; those that arrive by phone, email or without a notary stamp are not investigated, although senders are notified why their complaints have been summarily rejected. Anonymous written complaints are shredded.
 These rules can leave lawyers and litigants fearing retaliation, commission director Jenny Garrett noted in response to written questions.
 “It’s a ridiculous system that protects judges and makes it easy for them to intimidate anyone with a legitimate complaint,” said Sue Bell Cobb, chief justice of the Alabama Supreme Court from 2007 to 2011. In 2009, she unsuccessfully championed changes to the process and commissioned an American Bar Association report that offered a scathing review of Alabama’s rules.
 In most other states, commission staff members can start investigating a judge upon receiving a phone call or email, even anonymous ones, or after learning of questionable conduct from a news report or court filing. In Alabama, staff will not begin an investigation without approval from the commission itself, which convenes about every seven weeks.
  By rule, the commission also must keep a judge who is under scrutiny fully informed throughout an investigation. If a subpoena is issued, the judge receives a simultaneous copy, raising fears about witness intimidation. If a witness gives investigators a statement, the judge receives a transcript. In the U.S. justice system, such deference to individuals under investigation is extremely rare.
 “Why the need for special rules for judges?” said Michael Levy, a Washington lawyer who has represented clients in high-profile criminal, corporate, congressional and securities investigations. “If judges think it’s fair and appropriate to investigate others for crimes or misconduct without providing those subjects or targets with copies of witness statements and subpoenas, why don’t judges think it’s fair to investigate judges in the same way?”
 Alabama judges also are given an opportunity to resolve investigations confidentially. Reuters interviews and a review of Alabama commission records show the commission has met with judges informally at least 19 times since 2011 to offer corrective “guidance.” The identities of those judges remain confidential, as does the conduct that prompted the meetings. “Not every violation warrants discipline,” commission director Garrett said.
 Since 2008, the commission has brought 21 public cases against judges, including Hayes, charging two this year.
 Two of the best-known cases brought by the commission involved Roy Moore, who was twice forced out as chief justice of the Alabama Supreme Court for defying federal court orders.
 Another Alabama justice fared better in challenging a misconduct complaint, however. Tom Parker, first elected to the state’s high court in 2004, pushed back when the commission investigated him in 2015 for comments he made on the radio criticizing the U.S. Supreme Court’s decision legalizing gay marriage.
 Parker sued the commission in federal court, arguing the agency was infringing on his First Amendment rights. He won. Although the commission had dropped its investigation before the ruling, it was ordered to cover Parker’s legal fees: $100,000, or about a fifth of the agency’s total annual budget.
  These days, Parker told Reuters, Alabama judges and the agency that oversees them enjoy “a much better relationship” that’s less politically tinged. “How can I say it? It’s much more respectful between the commission and the judges now.”
  Montgomery, Alabama has a deep history of racial conflict, as reflected in the clashing concepts emblazoned on the city’s great seal: “Cradle of the Confederacy” and “Birthplace of the Civil Rights Movement.”
 Jefferson Davis was inaugurated here as Confederate president after the South seceded from the Union in 1861, and his birthday is a state holiday. As was common throughout the South, the city was the site of the lynchings of Black men, crimes now commemorated at a national memorial based here. Police arrested civil rights icon Rosa Parks here in 1955 for refusing to give up her seat on a city bus to a white passenger.
 Today, about 60% of Montgomery’s 198,000 residents are Black, U.S. census records show. Even so, Black motorists account for about 90% of those charged with unpaid traffic tickets, a Reuters examination of court records found. Much of Judge Hayes’ work in municipal court involved traffic cases and the collection of fines. Hayes, who is white, told Reuters that “the majority of people who come before the court are Black.”
 City officials have said that neither race nor economics have played a role in police efforts to enforce outstanding warrants, no matter how minor the offense.
 In April 2012, Marquita Johnson was among them. Appearing before Hayes on a Wednesday morning, the 28-year-old single mother pleaded for a break.
 Johnson had struggled for eight years to pay dozens of tickets that began with a citation for failing to show proof of insurance. She had insurance, she said. But when she was pulled over, she couldn’t find the card to prove it.
 Even a single ticket was a knockout blow on her minimum-wage waitress salary. In addition to fines, the court assessed a $155 fee to every ticket. Court records show that police often issued her multiple tickets for other infractions during every stop – a practice some residents call “stacking.”
 Under state law, failing to pay even one ticket can result in the suspension of a driver’s license. Johnson’s decision to keep driving nonetheless – taking her children to school or to doctor visits, getting groceries, going to work – led to more tickets and deeper debt.
 “I told Judge Hayes that I had lost my job and needed more time to pay,” she recounted.
 By Hayes’ calculation, Johnson owed more than $12,000 in fines. He sentenced Johnson to 496 days in jail. Hayes arrived at that sentence by counting each day in jail as $25 toward the outstanding debt. A different judge later determined that Johnson actually owed half the amount calculated by Hayes, and that Hayes had incorrectly penalized her over fines she had already paid. To shave time off her sentence, Johnson washed police cars and performed other menial labor while jailed.
 Hayes told Reuters that he generally found pleas of poverty hard to believe. “With my years of experience, I can tell when someone is being truthful with me,” Hayes said. He called it “gut instinct” - though he added, in a statement this week, that he also consulted “each defendant’s criminal and traffic history as well as their history of warrants and failures to appear in court.”
 Of course, the law demands more of a judge than a gut call. In a 1983 landmark decision, Bearden v. Georgia, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that state judges are obligated to hold a hearing to determine whether a defendant has “willfully” chosen not to pay a fine.
 According to the state’s judicial oversight commission, “Judge Hayes did not make any inquiry into Ms. Johnson’s ability to pay, whether her non-payment was willful.”
 From jail, “I prayed to return to my daughters,” Johnson said. “I was sure that someone would realize that Hayes had made a mistake.”
 She said her worst day in jail was her youngest daughter’s 3rd birthday. From a jail telephone, she tried to sing “Happy Birthday” but slumped to the floor in grief.
 “She was choking up and crying,” said Johnson’s mother, Blanche, who was on the call. “She was devastated to be away from her children so long.”
 When Johnson was freed after 10 months in jail, she learned that strangers had abused her two older children. One is now a teenager; the other is in middle school.
 “My kids will pay a lifetime for what the court system did to me,” Johnson said. “My daughters get frantic when I leave the house. I know they’ve had nightmares that I’m going to disappear again.”
 Six months after Johnson’s release, Hayes jailed another single Black mother. Angela McCullough, then 40, had been pulled over driving home from Faulkner University, a local community college where she carried a 3.87 grade point average. As a mother of four children, including a disabled adult son, she had returned to college to pursue her dream of becoming a mental health counselor.
 Police ticketed her for failing to turn on her headlights. After a background check,
......

title_en: Thousands of U.S. judges who broke laws, oaths remained on the bench

这是一个困扰科学家们多年的谜：当新的水坑和水池形成时，或者一个干燥孤立的水塘突然通过降雨或积雪融化而复活时，生活在这些水体中的鱼从哪里来？现在一项新的研究可能终于找到了答案：鸭子和粪便。
匈牙利研究人员将普鲁士和普通鲤鱼的活卵喂给了野鸭，并观察是否有活卵从另一端出来。普鲁士和鲤鱼都是入侵物种，在北美的水道中肆虐。他们发现，0.2%的鸡蛋经受住了通过鸟的消化道的旅程，并出现在它们新鲜的粪便中。6月22日，该研究小组在“美国国家科学院院刊”上报道，在这18个回收的卵中，有3个孵化成了鲤鱼宝宝，这表明绿头鸭，可能还有其他水禽，在远近的水体之间摆渡鱼卵。
多瑙河研究所匈牙利生态研究中心的生态学家、这项研究的合著者Orsolya Vincze说，由于绿头鸭数量丰富且分布广泛，即使比例很低，它们也可以移动大量的蛋，因为它们的数量巨大。(注：多瑙河研究所匈牙利生态研究中心的生态学家Orsolya Vincze是这项研究的合著者)。
绿头鸭在北美、亚洲和欧洲无处不在。这些浅水鸭以水生植物、蜗牛、昆虫和幼虫为食，在春季和初夏，它们与产卵的鲤鱼共享池塘、沼泽和湖边。一条普通鲤鱼每次产卵都会产下多达150万个小的粘性卵，而且它每个季节可以产卵多次。这些卵漂浮在水中或沉入浅滩，附着在沉水植物上。然后，野鸭大餐。
“鸟类就是喜欢鱼卵，”匈牙利多瑙河研究所生态研究中心的生态学家、这项研究的主要作者安达姆·洛瓦斯-基斯(Ardám Lovas-Kiss)说。“如果绿头鸭发现了这些产卵区，它们就会去那里吃掉这些蛋，直到它们动弹不得。对他们来说，这是一个很好的蛋白质资源。“。
Lovas-Kiss和他的团队想知道这些蛋中的一些在通过野鸭的酸性肠道时是否仍然活着和完好无损。以前的研究表明，一些植物种子可以在这次旅行中存活下来；绿头鸭在它们从一个湿地穿梭到另一个湿地时，把它们整个排泄了出来。Lovas-Kiss说：“我们试图弄清楚它们是否能清晰地排卵。”
在两个实验中，他的团队给圈养的八只绿头鸭各喂了500枚普通鲤鱼和普鲁士鲤鱼受精卵。研究人员之所以选择这些欧亚鱼种，是因为它们在过去几十年里被引入了世界上许多地方。使用放在鸭子笼子下的塑料托盘，研究小组在喂鸟的一天内收集了七次鸟类粪便，以取回完整的鸡蛋。结果发现，6只鸭子排出了18个完整的鱼卵，其中12个回收的卵存放着活胚胎。(剩下的六个蛋中的胚胎在通过野鸭的肠道时死亡。)。
接下来，研究人员将活卵放在装满河水的水族箱中，看看它们是否可以存活。他们发现，12枚蛋中有3枚--一枚普通鲤鱼和两枚普鲁士鲤鱼卵--在孵化的三天内孵化出来。
Vincze说，三条幼鱼看起来可能是一个很小的数字，但“如果你想想有多少水鸟，一条鱼能产多少蛋，它们能移动的蛋就很多了。”我认为这一切都合情合理。“。绿头鸭每天可以旅行15英里，在迁徙期间可以在两站之间飞行数百英里。这意味着它们可能会将卵分散到近处和远处的水体中。
这是一项初步但引人入胜的研究，“明尼苏达大学研究入侵鲤鱼的渔业生物学家彼得·索伦森(Peter Sorensen)说，他没有参与这项研究。我很想知道这些鱼卵是否能活到成年。“。
索伦森指出，普通鲤鱼幼体很少能在北美的湖泊中存活下来，因为捕食性鱼类会吃掉它们。“在我看来，卵被水禽传播并存活到成年的可能性并不是特别高。”但普鲁士鲤鱼可以无性繁殖，这意味着一个卵子就可以建立一个新的种群。他说：“普鲁士鲤鱼的风险似乎很明显，值得一看。”
为了进一步支持鸭子帮助将鱼摆渡到新的水体的理论，研究人员现在正寻求用其他几种鱼的卵重复这一实验，看看它们是否像鲤鱼卵一样，在绿头鸭的肠道中存活下来，并成功孵化和发育。如果他们这样做了，Vincze说，“这将解决几个世纪以来一直没有得到回答的问题。”
note_en:
It's a mystery that has plagued scientists for ages: When new puddles and pools form, or a dry and isolated pond suddenly gets revived through rainfall or snowmelt, where do the fish that live in these waterbodies comes from? Now a new study might have finally found the answer: duck poop.
 Hungarian researchers fed live eggs of Prussian and common carp—both invasive fish species wreaking havoc in North America’s waterways—to Mallards and looked to see whether any came out the other end. They found that 0.2 percent of eggs endured the journey through the bird’s digestive tract and showed up in their fresh feces. Three of those 18 recovered eggs hatched into baby carps, the team reported on June 22 in  Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, suggesting that Mallards, and possibly other waterfowl, ferry fish eggs between waterbodies near and far.
 Because Mallards are abundant and widespread,  even with that low percentage, “they can move a lot of eggs because of their huge numbers,” says Orsolya Vincze, an ecologist at Danube Research Institute’s Centre for Ecological Research in Hungary and co-author of the study.
 Mallards are ubiquitous across North America, Asia, and Europe. These shallow-water ducks feed on aquatic plants, snails, insects, and larvae, and in spring and early summer they share their ponds, marshes, and lake edges with spawning carp. A single common carp lays up to 1.5 million small, sticky eggs every time it spawns, and it can spawn multiple times per season. These eggs float in the water or sink to the shallows and attach to submerged plants. Then, Mallards feast.
 “Birds just love fish eggs,” says Ádám Lovas-Kiss, an ecologist at Danube Research Institute’s Centre for Ecological Research in Hungary and lead author of the study. “If Mallards find these spawning areas, they will go there and eat the eggs until they can’t move. It’s a great resource for protein for them.”
 Lovas-Kiss and his team wanted to know if some of these eggs remain alive and intact as they pass through the Mallard’s acidic gut. Previous  studies have shown that some plant seeds can survive this journey; Mallards pooped them out whole as they shuttled from one wetland to the next . “We tried to figure out if they can defecate the eggs viably,” Lovas-Kiss says.
 In two experiments, his team fed 500 fertilized eggs of common and Prussian carp to each of eight Mallards housed in captivity. The researchers chose these Eurasian fish species because they’ve been introduced to many parts of the world in the last few decades. Using a plastic tray placed under the ducks’ cage, the team collected bird droppings seven times within a day of feeding the birds to retrieve intact eggs. Turns out, six ducks pooped out 18 intact fish eggs, and 12 of those recovered eggs housed live embryos. (The embryos within the remaining six eggs died as they passed through the Mallards’ gut.)
  Next, the researchers placed the live eggs in aquaria filled with river water to see if they were viable. They found that three of the 12 eggs—one common carp and two Prussian carp eggs—hatched within three days of incubation.
 Three baby fish may seem like a small number, Vincze says, but “if you think about how many water birds there are and how many eggs one fish can produce, it’s a lot of eggs that they can move. I think it all adds up.” Mallards can travel up to  15 miles each day and fly  several hundred miles between stops during migration. That means they could possibly disperse eggs to waterbodies near and far.
 "It's a preliminary but fascinating study,” says Peter Sorensen, a fisheries biologist studying invasive carps at the University of Minnesota who was not involved in the research. "I would be interested to know if those fish eggs survive to adulthood.”
 Sorensen points out that common carp hatchlings rarely survive in North America’s lakes because predatory fish eat them. “The chances of eggs being spread by waterfowl and surviving through adulthood doesn’t seem especially high to me.” But Prussian carp can reproduce asexually, which means a single egg could establish a new population. “The risk for Prussian carp does seem notable and it would be worth looking at,” he says.
 To further support the theory that ducks help ferry fish to new waterbodies, the researchers are now looking to repeat the experiment with eggs of several other fish species to see if they, like the carp eggs, survive the Mallard’s gut and successfully hatch and develop. If they do, Vincze says, “it would solve the question that’s remained unanswered for centuries.”
title: 绿头鸭通过粪便在水体之间摆渡鱼卵
title_en: Mallards Ferry Fish Eggs Between Waterbodies Through Their Poop

note:
Facebook Reality Labs(FRL)一直在探索新的光学架构，以提高外形、舒适性和光学性能。去年秋天，在Oculus Connect 6上，FRL首席科学家Michael Abrash介绍了半穹顶2和3在VR方面的新微型化进展，这两个原型研究了变焦显示器如何改善视觉和身体舒适性。今年，在虚拟SIGGRAPH大会上，我们将展示这条道路上的另一个研究里程碑：一种新的光学架构，它明显更紧凑，并提供了更好的视觉性能的潜力。
在这项名为“用于薄而轻的虚拟现实的全息光学”的工作中，研究人员Andrew Maimone和Junren Wang提出了一种新的近眼显示器，它结合了全息光学的力量和基于偏振的光学折叠-这种方法可以用来开发未来类似太阳镜的VR硬件。这两种方法有助于使光学元件尽可能薄，同时最有效地利用空间。我们预计，这种轻巧舒适的外形规格可能会延长VR会话时间，增加新的使用案例，包括生产力。
该设计在一种概念验证研究设备中进行了演示，该设备仅使用薄而平的薄膜作为光学元件，以实现小于9 mm的显示厚度，同时支持可与当今消费性VR产品相媲美的视野。这项工作也展示了更好的视觉性能的前景：激光照明被用来向VR显示器提供更广泛的色域，并且在将分辨率扩展到人类视觉极限方面取得了进展。
这张图片显示了我们的研究设备显示模块安装在一个框架中。此研究设备用于捕获如下所示的绿色图像(某些组件安装在外部)。
今天的VR显示器具有三个主要组件：光源(例如，LED)、使光变亮或变暗以形成图像的显示面板(例如，LCD面板)、以及将图像聚焦到足够远使得观看者的眼睛可以看到它的观察光学组件(例如，塑料透镜)。由于前两个组件可以很容易地形成薄而平的模块，因此大部分重量和体积都进入了观察光学系统。为了显著降低VR显示器的整体尺寸和重量，我们结合了两种技术：全息光学和基于偏振的光学折叠。
大多数VR显示器共用一种常见的观察光学系统：由厚厚的曲线片或玻璃或塑料组成的简单折射透镜。我们建议用全息光学代替这种庞大的元件。你可能熟悉在科学博物馆或你的信用卡上看到的全息图像，它们看起来是三维的，页面内外都有真实的深度。就像这些全息图像一样，我们的全息光学是激光与物体相互作用的记录，但在这种情况下，物体是透镜，而不是3D场景。其结果是厚度和重量大幅降低：全息光学像镜头一样弯曲光线，但看起来像一张薄而透明的贴纸。
然而，即使镜头本身变薄，观察光学系统作为一个整体可能仍然很大-在显示面板和镜头之间必须放置相当大的空白空间才能正确对焦图像。通常，来自显示面板的光向前传播到镜头，然后继续朝向眼睛。然而，当我们应用基于偏振的光学折叠时，可以控制光在镜头内向前和向后移动，以便可以多次穿过这个空白空间，将其折叠到原始体积的一小部分。
左边显示的是用上图所示的概念验证研究设备拍摄的照片。右边是一张通过较大的全彩色台式电脑原型拍摄的照片。我们目前正致力于在较小的研究原型上实现全彩色。
当我们将全息光学应用于VR显示器时，我们必须重新评估所有其他光学元件。值得注意的是，全息光学迫使使用激光光源，激光光源更难集成，但提供的颜色集比当今几乎所有VR耳机、手机、计算机和电视中常见的LED丰富得多。
为了说明不同之处，下图显示了人类可见颜色的色域。目前在许多显示器上可以重现的一组常见颜色是sRGB颜色空间(由较小的三角形说明)。请注意，它只能捕捉我们实际能看到的一小部分颜色。相比之下，外三角形代表了更大的一组颜色，可以使用激光在我们的一个研究原型显示器上重现。这允许再现鲜艳和饱和的颜色。想想一个灯火通明的霓虹灯招牌，或者蝴蝶翅膀的彩虹般的光泽。
此图说明了人类可见颜色的色域。sRGB空间代表了当今许多显示器上可重现的一组常见颜色。外三角形代表了我们的研究原型上可重现的更大的一组颜色。
虽然它指出了轻量化、舒适性和高性能AR/VR技术的未来发展，但目前我们的工作还只是纯粹的研究。在我们的技术论文中，我们确定了我们建议的显示体系结构目前的局限性，并讨论了使该方法更实用的未来研究领域。据我们所知，我们的工作展示了迄今为止展示的最薄的VR显示屏，我们很高兴看到未来会发生什么。
note_en:
Facebook Reality Labs (FRL) is always exploring new optical architectures to improve form factor, comfort, and optical performance. Last fall, at Oculus Connect 6, FRL Chief Scientist Michael Abrash   introduced new miniaturization progress in VR with   Half Dome 2 and 3, two prototypes that examine how varifocal displays can improve visual and physical comfort. This year, at the   virtual SIGGRAPH conference, we’re presenting another research milestone on this path: a new optical architecture that is significantly more compact and offers the potential for better visual performance.
 In this work, “ Holographic Optics for Thin and Lightweight Virtual Reality,” researchers Andrew Maimone and Junren Wang propose a new class of near-eye displays, which combine the power of holographic optics and polarization-based optical folding — an approach that could be used to develop future sunglasses-like VR hardware. These two methods help keep the optics as thin as possible while making the most efficient use of space. We anticipate that such lightweight and comfortable form factors may enable extended VR sessions and new use cases, including productivity.
 The design is demonstrated in a proof-of-concept research device that uses only thin, flat films as optics to achieve a display thickness of less than 9 mm while supporting a field of view comparable to today’s consumer VR products. The work demonstrates the promise of better visual performance, as well: Laser illumination is used to deliver a much wider gamut of colors to VR displays, and progress is made toward scaling resolution to the limit of human vision.
     This image shows our research device display modules mounted into a frame. This research device was used to capture the green image shown below (some components are mounted externally).
 Today’s VR displays have three primary components: a source of light (e.g., LEDs), a display panel that brightens or dims the light to form an image (e.g., an LCD panel), and a viewing optic that focuses the image far enough away so that the viewer’s eyes can see it (e.g., a plastic lens). As the first two components can readily be formed into thin and flat modules, most of the weight and volume go into the viewing optics. To significantly reduce the overall size and weight of VR displays, we combine two techniques: holographic optics and polarization-based optical folding.
  Most VR displays share a common viewing optic: a simple refractive lens composed of a thick, curved piece or glass or plastic. We propose replacing this bulky element with holographic optics. You may be familiar with holographic images seen at a science museum or on your credit card, which appear to be three-dimensional with realistic depth in or out of the page. Like these holographic images, our holographic optics are a recording of the interaction of laser light with objects, but in this case the object is a lens rather than a 3D scene. The result is a dramatic reduction in thickness and weight: The holographic optic bends light like a lens but looks like a thin, transparent sticker.
 However, even if the lens itself is made thin, the viewing optics as a whole may still be large — a considerable amount of empty space must be placed between the display panel and the lens to properly focus the image. Ordinarily, light from the display panel propagates forward to the lens and then continues toward the eye. However, when we apply polarization-based optical folding, light can be controlled to move both forward and backward within the lens so that this empty space can be traversed multiple times, collapsing it to a fraction of the original volume.
   Shown on the left, a photograph captured with the proof-of-concept research device shown above. On the right, a photograph taken through a larger full-color benchtop prototype. We are currently working on achieving full color on the smaller research prototype.
 When we apply holographic optics to a VR display, we must reevaluate all other optical components. Notably, holographic optics compel the use of laser light sources, which are more difficult to integrate but provide a much richer set of colors than the LEDs common in nearly all of today’s VR headsets, phones, computers, and televisions.
 To illustrate the difference, the figure below shows the gamut of human-visible colors. A common set of colors reproducible on many displays today is the sRGB color space (illustrated by the smaller triangle). Note that it can capture only a small fraction of the colors that we can actually see. In contrast, the outer triangle represents the much larger set of colors that can be reproduced using the lasers on one of our research prototype displays. This allows the reproduction of vivid and saturated colors. Think of a brightly lit neon sign or the iridescent sheen of a butterfly wing.
  This figure illustrates the gamut of human-visible colors. The sRGB space represents a common set of colors reproducible on many displays today. The outer triangle represents the larger set of colors reproducible on our research prototype.
  While it points toward the future development of lightweight, comfortable, and high-performance AR/VR technology, at present our work is purely research. In our technical paper, we identify the current limitations of our proposed display architecture and discuss future areas of research that will make the approach more practical. To our knowledge, our work demonstrates the thinnest VR display demonstrated to date, and we’re excited to see what the future holds.
title: 用于轻薄虚拟现实的全息光学
title_en: Holographic optics for thin and lightweight virtual reality

note:
加州大学旧金山分校的冠状病毒专家鲍勃·瓦克特博士周一敲响了加州冠状病毒病例激增的警钟。
加州大学旧金山分校医学系主任瓦克特在推特上写道。直到最近，我们的州都成功地做到了这一点，我们的经历被称为加州奇迹。但让我们明确地说：奇迹已经结束了。我们越早承认这一点，我们的境况就越好。
瓦赫特通过在推特上定期发布冗长的帖子来解开复杂的事情，并提供内部人士对这场流行病的看法，从而成为一名公认的新冠肺炎专家。
在他周一的帖子中，有24条推文，Wachter讲述了加州如何从一个传播速度非常低的州变成了医院床位迅速填满的州。总体而言，过去的两周是惨淡的：新病例翻了一番，住院↑为43%，重症监护病房患者↑为37%，&amp；检测阳性率从4.4%→为5.5%。
1/Covid(@UCSF)编年史，第104天让我们谈谈加州。直到最近，我们的州都成功地做到了这一点，我们的经历被称为加州奇迹。但让我们明确地说：奇迹已经结束了。我们越早承认这一点，我们的境况就越好。
-Bob Wachter(@Bob_Wachter)2020年6月30日。
Wachter指出了其他案件激增的州，如德克萨斯州、亚利桑那州和佛罗里达州，他写道，人们可以很容易地指出州长和其他政客的领导不端行为。他们散布了关于Covid的错误信息，但没有要求采取以证据为基础的做法，如掩饰，以防止传播。不可饶恕。&#34；
他说，加州正在经历一种不同的情况。瓦赫特写道：我们的领导人采取了负责任的行动。加州人大多也是如此。但在阵亡将士纪念日前后，标准放松了，人们(是的，主要是年轻人)变得自满，在一连串的幸运突破之后，我们的运气用完了。现在是我们的时代了。
正如许多专家得出的结论，Wachter指出，该州在大流行中及早采取的行动导致了3月、4月和5月的低传播率。
但我们安然无恙地度过了3个月，他写道。截至6月1日，加利福尼亚州总共有约10万例病例和4000例死亡。相比之下，拥有一半人口的纽约州约有37万病例&3万人死亡。瓦克特写道，“加州奇迹”的叙述诞生了；我促成了它。
自那以后，故事发生了变化，瓦克特说，预计病例数量会增加，但包括他在内，几乎没有人预料到这种情况会如此迅速和广泛。
因此，他写道，即使是在加利福尼亚州，有了掩饰和疏远的命令，许多人也变得马虎了。最重要的是，我们的幸运之旅戛然而止。一个可怕的例子是旧金山北部的圣昆廷监狱，那里感染病毒的囚犯人数刚刚超过1000人。
Wachter指出，旧金山还没有看到加州其他县正在经历的激增，但这种情况可能会改变。他写道，不是激增，而是警示信号。&#34；@UCSF医院16分，5分在通风口；~2周前翻了一番。在SF，病例数和住院率都在上升，住院病人中的BUMP大部分是从外地转来的。请原谅我的比喻，但如果有人在洛杉矶打喷嚏，我们最终会在旧金山感冒。
州长加文·纽瑟姆(Gavin Newsom)在周一的新闻发布会上也对加州的飙升表示震惊。瓦克特指出，州长传达的信息非常明确：如果我们不扭转局面，我们可能很快就会发现自己又回到了广泛的原地避难所。
title: 奇迹结束了：加州大学旧金山分校的医生谈加州出了什么问题
title_en: 'The miracle is over': UCSF doctor on what went wrong in California

note:
香港--随着北京为香港实施新的国家安全法的时间紧迫，香港人正争先恐后地改变他们的数字足迹，或者完全从社交媒体上删除他们的存在。
甚至在这项立法预计于周二获得通过之前，它就已经在香港人中引发了广泛的自我审查。
周一，一位名为Winnie&34；的推特用户停用了一个标签为#香港抗议的账户。这条推文说，我不这么做是因为我觉得自己处于危险之中，这是出于高度的谨慎，并补充说，我的心与那些看得见的活动家们在一起。
周一，亲民主的《苹果日报》(Apple Daily)创始人黎智英在他的认证推特账户上写道，现居美国的前天安门学生领袖王丹曾警告他和著名活动人士黄志峰，他们将是安全法律颁布后的头两个目标。但我不会离开#香港，#34；赖写道。
新法律将宣布分裂主义、颠覆、恐怖主义和外国干涉为非法活动，并在去年的大规模反政府抗议活动之后出台，这引起了法律专家的担忧。
香港大学(University Of Hong Kong)法学教授约翰·陈(Johannes Chan)在一封电子邮件中告诉“日经亚洲评论”(Nikkei Asian Review)，拟议中的罪行的确切范围和内容尚不清楚。尽管北京和香港的官员保证，这项法律只会影响一小部分人，但谁来决定少数人的问题仍然没有答案。
陈冯富珍表示，一旦你属于少数派，“基本法”或普通法体系中的任何人权保障都不会适用。陈冯富珍指的是香港的小宪法，它是一国两制框架的一部分。他说，很明显，这项法律将严重影响言论自由，如果不是人身安全的话，也会对香港人造成严重影响。
他说，甚至在国家安全法颁布之前，它就已经产生了影响。虽然香港的一些公司表达了他们对新法律的支持，但许多人已经开始删除他们在Facebook上的帖子。
一位不愿透露姓名的快30岁的高中教师向日经解释了他最近在Facebook上改名的原因：我们现在非常害怕我们会被所谓的在线朋友出卖。
他还更改了社交媒体上的头像，删除了政治敏感帖子，并查看了他的网上好友名单，看看是否有人可能会因为他在政治和社会问题上的自由主义观点而攻击他。他通过阅读他们最近发布的政治观点来筛选他的在线联系人。
他说，有些人利用这些内容作为武器来投诉我。这是一种保护我们自己的方式，也是防止来自网络的各种攻击和骚扰的一种方式。
这位老师的同事也要求匿名，他也采取了类似的方法。这位30岁出头的同事在得知其他人出于安全考虑而这样做后，更改了他使用了七年的网名。
这位同事不仅放弃了他熟悉的网络空间身份，删除了过去可能被认为是政治敏感的帖子，还停止了对Facebook和Instagram上发布的新闻和社交活动的评论。他现在只是简单地转发他在网上找到的内容，而不做额外的评论。
这位同事说，有这么多亲北京的网民积极骚扰和举报其他持亲民主政治观点的网民。在香港，拥有自由和民主观点的教师使他们很容易受到当局可能的攻击，他们可能会面临失业，就像他们的一些同龄人已经发生的那样。
两人都说恐惧笼罩着他们，因为国家安全法的颁布迫在眉睫。尽管法律的条款和条款尚未公布，但香港亲北京知名人士的言论以及中国官方的新华社的一篇摘要在香港许多人中引起了紧张。
根除和改变数字足迹的做法最早出现在去年6月，当时爆发了一系列抗议活动，抗议一项现已撤回的引渡法案。自影响深远的国家安全法出台以来，此类举措加快了步伐。
香港中文大学新闻学教授克莱门特·Y·K·苏(Clement Y.K.So)告诉日经，这项法律不仅对记者，而且对整个香港人都有进一步的影响。人们开始真正担心起来，他们开始删除过去在网上说过的话，他说。
在新法律出台之前，SO&#的一些朋友也已经改名，删除了网络空间中的内容，并关闭了WhatsApp聊天群。他们不愿透露身份。这是对这项法律颁布的直接反应，他说。
今年4月，当香港最大的航空公司国泰航空(Cathay Pacific Airways)推出一个内部在线平台，供员工举报和投诉同事时，该航空公司的许多员工决定删除他们的社交媒体账户。
一位空乘人员说：你永远不知道哪些同事会报告你的情况。她说：我们不再谈论政治了，并补充说，同事之间的不信任感是前所未有的。
据国泰航空工会称，自去年香港爆发骚乱以来，已有约40名员工因在社交媒体上发布支持抗议的帖子而被解雇。该公司前首席执行官鲁珀特·霍格(Rupert Hogg)去年8月辞职，原因是中国大陆监管机构批评他对参与抗议活动的员工采取了宽大的立场。
随着国家安全法即将出台，这位空姐表示，在香港其他行业工作的人将面临她现在面临的同样压力。她说：有如此多的中国公司在这里运营，中国是许多企业的主要市场。有多少公司能够承受失去这个市场的代价？
我没有做错什么，20多岁的老师说。我所做的只是谈论、分享或表达我对社会和政治事件的看法，这并不违法，也不会对社会上的任何人造成伤害。他承认，在抗议期间，他是比较顽固地保留自己原始网络身份的人之一，直到他的朋友们多次劝说他出于安全考虑，才最终说服他改变了自己的网络个人资料。
他的同事也是这样。分享我对特定新闻的看法是我的惯常做法。我不在乎，因为香港是一个你想说什么就说什么的地方。但现在，他说，那似乎已经是过去的一部分了。我们曾经生活在一个自由的世界里，没有恐惧……。现在，我真的生活在恐惧中。
即使两位教师暂时不打算离开香港，但这仍然是一个选择。这位20多岁的老师说，在国家安全法案最初公布后，他与父亲和弟弟进行了交谈，并决定留在香港，尽管这家人有经济能力离开。
他说，离开是投降的标志，并补充说，这将向政府将亲民主居民和持不同政见者推向国外的意图让步。我想继续战斗和抵抗。
他的同事表示，他在经济上没有能力离开香港，但当这位有史以来第一位没有影响力的无足轻重的香港人因违反法律而被捕时，他仍将准备离开。在此之前，他誓言将继续挑衅。
对于他来说，完全顺从太令人窒息了，他拒绝成为生活在动物农场的另一只动物，将其与乔治·奥威尔(George Orwell)的经典小说相提并论，在这部小说中，统治着动物的猪逐渐将一个动物群落变成了一个独裁的世界。
这位同事说：我知道它正在减少，但我会尽我所能。
他说，保持挑衅是对付威权政权的最好、也是唯一的办法。如果你失去了精神，就没有希望。

我最近花了相当多的时间和布莱泽一起玩。其中一个好处是，使用WebAssembly，您可以生成一个静态网站，并将其免费托管在GitHub页面上。最重要的是，整个过程可以通过GitHub操作实现自动化，因此您不必担心。
这不是一个复杂的过程，总而言之只需要几个步骤。只有一个警告：如果您的存储库是“标准”用户或组织存储库，则只能从主分支部署到页面。
用户和组织站点的默认发布源是主站和分站。如果您的用户或组织站点的存储库有一个主站点分支，则您的站点将自动从该分支发布。您不能为用户或组织网站选择不同的发布源。
否则，如果您的存储库属于某个项目，您可以将其配置为从其他分支部署：
项目站点的默认发布源是较高页面的分支。如果您的项目站点的存储库有一个超长页面的分支，则您的站点将自动从该分支发布。
项目站点也可以从MASTER MASTER分支或MASTER MASTER分支上的/Docs文件夹发布。
那么我们要怎么做才能看到我们漂亮的网站呢？它的核心都在这里：
名称：GH-Pages：[Push]作业：Build：Run-On：Ubuntu-最新步骤：-用法：Actions/Checkout@v2-Name：Setup.NET核心用法：Actions/Setup-dotnet@v1 with：dotnet-version：3.1.301-name：使用dotnet发布运行：dotnet Publish--配置发布--输出build-name：Deploy to Github Pages用法：JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@releases/v3 with：Access_Token：${Secrets.ACCESS_。BRANCH：MASTER#操作应该部署到的分支。Folder：build/wwwroot#操作应该部署的文件夹。Single_Commit：TRUE
也没有什么东西可以添加到应用程序代码中。首先，将此javascript片段添加到index.html：
&lt；！--启动GitHub页面的单页应用--&gt；&lt；script type=&#34；text/javascript&#34；&gt；//GitHub页面单页应用//https://github.com/rafrex/spa-github-pages//版权所有(C)2016年Rafael Pedicini，根据麻省理工学院的许可//--//此脚本检查查询字符串中是否存在重定向//并将其转换回正确的URL并将其添加到//浏览器&#39；使用window.history.replaceState(.)的历史记录，//这不会导致浏览器尝试加载新的url。//在此文件中进一步加载单页应用程序时，//正确的URL将在浏览器的历史记录中等待//单页应用程序进行相应的路由。(Function(L){if(l.search){var q={}；l.search.slice(1).split(&#39；&amp；&#39；).forEach(function(V){var a=v.Split(&#39；=&#39；)；q[a[0]]=a.slice(1).join(&#39；=&#39；).replace(/~and~/g，&#39；&amp；&#39；)；})；如果(q.p！==未定义){window.History y.replaceState(null，null，l.pathname.Slice(0，-1)+(q.p||&#39；&#39；&#39；+q.q)：&#39；&#39；)+l.hash)；}(window.location)&lt；/script&gt；&lt；！--end。
正如您从注释中看到的，此代码帮助处理URL和重定向。
因此，如果名称为BlazorOnGitHubPages，最终的网址将类似于https://mizrael.github.io/BlazorOnGitHubPages/(恰好就是我正在使用的网址，请随意尝试)。
下一步是在/wwwroot文件夹中添加一个404.html页面和一个空的.nojekyll文件。GitHub页面是使用Jekyll构建的，它不构建任何以_开头的内容。但是，Blazor会在/wwwroot中生成一个_framework子文件夹，正如您可以想象的那样，它非常重要。
note_en:
I have been spending  quite some time lately playing with  Blazor. One of the nice things is that with  webassembly you can generate a static website and have it hosted on   GitHub Pages for free. Most importantly, the whole process can be automated with  GitHub Actions so you don’t have to worry about it.
 It’s not a complicated process, all in all just a few steps. There is only one caveat: if your repository is a “standard” User or Organization repository, you can deploy to Pages only from the  master branch.
  The default publishing source for user and organization sites is the  master branch. If the repository for your user or organization site has a  master branch, your site will publish automatically from that branch. You cannot choose a different publishing source for user or organization sites.
 Otherwise, if your repository belongs to a  Project, you can configure it to deploy from a different branch:
 The default publishing source for a project site is the  gh-pages branch. If the repository for your project site has a  gh-pages branch, your site will publish automatically from that branch.
 Project sites can also be published from the  master branch or a  /docs folder on the  master branch.
 So what do we have to do in order to see our nice website? The core  it’s all here:
 name: gh-pageson: [push]jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v2 - name: Setup .NET Core uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v1 with: dotnet-version: 3.1.301 - name: Publish with dotnet run: dotnet publish --configuration Release --output build - name: Deploy to Github Pages uses: JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@releases/v3 with: ACCESS_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.ACCESS_TOKEN }} BASE_BRANCH: development # The branch the action should deploy from. BRANCH: master # The branch the action should deploy to. FOLDER: build/wwwroot # The folder the action should deploy. SINGLE_COMMIT: true
   There are also few things to add to the application code as well. First of all, add this javascript snippet to your  index.html:
 <!-- Start Single Page Apps for GitHub Pages --> <script type="text/javascript"> // Single Page Apps for GitHub Pages // https://github.com/rafrex/spa-github-pages // Copyright (c) 2016 Rafael Pedicini, licensed under the MIT License // ---------------------------------------------------------------------- // This script checks to see if a redirect is present in the query string // and converts it back into the correct url and adds it to the // browser's history using window.history.replaceState(...), // which won't cause the browser to attempt to load the new url. // When the single page app is loaded further down in this file, // the correct url will be waiting in the browser's history for // the single page app to route accordingly. (function (l) { if (l.search) { var q = {}; l.search.slice(1).split('&').forEach(function (v) { var a = v.split('='); q[a[0]] = a.slice(1).join('=').replace(/~and~/g, '&'); }); if (q.p !== undefined) { window.history.replaceState(null, null, l.pathname.slice(0, -1) + (q.p || '') + (q.q ? ('?' + q.q) : '') + l.hash ); } } }(window.location)) </script> <!-- End Single Page Apps for GitHub Pages -->
 As you can see from the comment, this code helps to handle URLs and redirections.
   So if the name is   BlazorOnGitHubPages, the final URL will be something like    https://mizrael.github.io/BlazorOnGitHubPages/ (which happens to be the one I’m using, feel free to try it).
 The next step is to add a  404.html page and an empty  .nojekyll file in the  /wwwroot folder. GitHub Pages are built using Jekyll and it does not build anything that starts with _ . Blazor, however, generates a  _framework subfolder inside  /wwwroot and as you can imagine, it’s  quite important.
title: 如何使用GitHub操作在GitHub页面上部署Blazor WebAssembly
title_en: How to Deploy Blazor WebAssembly on GitHub Pages Using GitHub Actions

伊朗对特朗普总统发出逮捕令，该逮捕令与1月3日美国无人机袭击造成少将卡西姆·索莱马尼(Qassem Soleimani)死亡有关。
德黑兰检察官阿里·阿尔卡西梅尔(Ali Alqasimehr)星期一宣布了这一消息，称特朗普是伊朗政府希望因在伊拉克杀害索莱马尼而受审的30多名嫌疑人之一。
伊朗检察官没有透露他们希望看到因索莱马尼之死而受审的数十名其他嫌疑人的名字。
索莱马尼和其他伊朗官员在巴格达国际机场附近的无人机袭击中丧生。
美国国防部在暗杀事件发生后表示：“在总统的指示下，美国军方采取了果断的防御行动，以保护在海外的美国人员。”
伊朗发誓要进行“严厉报复”，几天后向伊拉克的两个美军基地发射了十几枚导弹，据报道造成100多名士兵脑部受伤。
国际刑警组织拒绝了伊朗要求参与将特朗普送上法庭的请求。国际警察机构表示，它不会介入“政治”性质的事务。
美国驻伊朗特使布莱恩·胡克(Brian Hook)对伊朗的指控不屑一顾，称其为“没有人认真对待的宣传噱头”。

Iran has issued an arrest warrant for President Trump tied to the Jan. 3 U.S. drone strike that killed major general Qassem Soleimani.
  Tehran prosecutor Ali Alqasimehr made the announcement Monday, stating that Trump is one of more than 30 suspects Iran’s government wants to put on trial for the killing of Soleimani in Iraq.
      Iranian prosecutors did not name the dozens of other suspects they want to see tried for Soleimani’s death.
        Soleimani, along with other Iranian officials, was  killed by a drone strike near Baghdad International Airport.
  “At the direction of the president, the U.S. military has taken decisive defensive action to protect U.S. personnel abroad,” the U.S. Department of Defense  stated after the assassination.
      Vowing “harsh retaliation,” Iran  fired more than a dozen  missiles at two U.S. military bases in Iraq days later,  reportedly causing brain injuries to more than 100 soldiers.
    Interpol has declined Iran’s request to get involved in bringing Trump to trial. The international policing agency said it does not get involved in matters of a “political” nature.
        U.S. envoy to Iran Brian Hook dismissed Iran’s charges as “a propaganda stunt that no-one takes seriously.”
  Iran reportedly intends to pursue charges against Trump when and if he returns to civilian life.
  An  ABC News poll taken after the airstrike that killed Soleimani indicated that 53% of Americans approved of Trump’s decision.
